coredata.dc:title: An innovative synthesis of (Ag0-pullulan-Ag+) as an optical nanoprobe for sensitive and selective detection of Fe2+ ion based on redox reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Jayeoye, T.J.
Eze, F.N.
Muangsin, N.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we Synthesized an efficacious optical nanoprobe (Ag0-pullulan-Ag+) for sensitive and discriminative detection of ferrous ions (Fe2+) over ferric ions (Fe3+) in solution. Pullulan (PUL) biopolymer was used as the reductant of excess AgNO3, at moderate heating, so that stable and dispersed silver nanoparticles (AgNPs or Ag0) were formed, which also contained an unreduced silver ion (Ag+), all intertwined in the pullulan polymeric matrix. Extensive characterization confirmed the role of PUL as a capping agent, while sharp Ag0 and Ag+ peaks were validated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The Ag0-PUL-Ag+ probe showed a sharp absorption maximum at 415 nm, having a majority size distribution around 20–30 nm, from TEM. The addition of Ag0-PUL-Ag+, acetate buffer pH 5.0, and Fe2+ under room temperature incubation led to Fe2+ reduction of available Ag+ to more AgNPs, based on redox reaction, with color progression from light yellow to yellow, orange, and brownish yellow. The ΔA@411 plotted against Fe2+ concentration was linear within 1.0–20.0 and 20.0–125.0 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.15 μM. The assay was selective to only Fe2+ over other metal ions and was applied to Fe2+ detection in tap water and in ferrous fumarate tablets with satisfactory accuracy/precision. Our work showed that with careful control of nanomaterial synthesis conditions, a sensitive detection probe could be obtained for impressive analyte sensing/detection.

(a) TEM images (b) dominant particle size distribution from TEM of Ag0-PUL-Ag+, FESEM images of (c) PUL film (d) Ag0-PUL-Ag+ (e) ATR-FTIR and (f) Raman spectra of the blue line (PUL) and red line (Ag0-PUL-Ag+).

Ag0-PUL-Ag+ after the addition of treated ferrous fumarate tablet (unspiked), Ag0-PUL-Ag+ after the addition of c. head.ce:title: An innovative synthesis of (Ag0-pullulan-Ag+) as an optical nanoprobe for sensitive and selective detection of Fe2+ ion based on redox reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Delivery Systems Excellence Center, Faculty of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Prince of Songkhla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90112, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Drug Delivery Systems Excellence Center, Faculty of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Prince of Songkhla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90112, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we Synthesized an efficacious optical nanoprobe (Ag0-pullulan-Ag+) for sensitive and discriminative detection of ferrous ions (Fe2+) over ferric ions (Fe3+) in solution. Pullulan (PUL) biopolymer was used as the reductant of excess AgNO3, at moderate heating, so that stable and dispersed silver nanoparticles (AgNPs or Ag0) were formed, which also contained an unreduced silver ion (Ag+), all intertwined in the pullulan polymeric matrix. Extensive characterization confirmed the role of PUL as a capping agent, while sharp Ag0 and Ag+ peaks were validated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The Ag0-PUL-Ag+ probe showed a sharp absorption maximum at 415 nm, having a majority size distribution around 20–30 nm, from TEM. The addition of Ag0-PUL-Ag+, acetate buffer pH 5.0, and Fe2+ under room temperature incubation led to Fe2+ reduction of available Ag+ to more AgNPs, based on redox reaction, with color progression from light yellow to yellow, orange, and brownish yellow. The ΔA@411 plotted against Fe2+ concentration was linear within 1.0–20.0 and 20.0–125.0 μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.15 μM. The assay was selective to only Fe2+ over other metal ions and was applied to Fe2+ detection in tap water and in ferrous fumarate tablets with satisfactory accuracy/precision. Our work showed that with careful control of nanomaterial synthesis conditions, a sensitive detection probe could be obtained for impressive analyte sensing/detection.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Pullulan stabilized/reduced AgNPs realized at optimum synthesis conditions

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Discriminative naked eye/colorimetric detection of Fe2+ over Fe3+ ions in an aqueous environment has always been challenging. This is attributed to the close chemical reactivity of the two iron states with ligands on nanoparticles’ surfaces. For example, Fe3+-specific binding with catechol hydroxyl groups of dopamine has been well exploited for designing dopamine assays [1]; however, this was later discounted by the work of An et al. who found that indeed Fe2+ does interact with dopamine derivatives [2]. Moreover, a detailed review of reported works for Fe2+ detection showed that only very few works tested the selectivity of the reported assay towards Fe2+, even in the presence of Fe3+, owing to the afore mentioned similarity in chemical reactivity. Consequently, the design of a colorimetric assay for Fe2+ detection over Fe3+ (even at higher concentrations), is of utmost importance to fill up such a research gap. The interplay between the two major oxidation states of iron (Fe2+ and Fe3+) is crucial to the optimal performance of diverse metabolic activities concerned with electron and oxygen transport in the cells. For example, Fe2+ is the preferred state for hemoglobin (an oxygen-carrying protein within the cells), while the binding of Fe3+ resulting from the oxidation of Fe2+ is referred to as methemoglobin. This manifests as a low oxygen level in the blood, which may lead to cyanosis [3]. Fe2+ is a co-factor for some important enzymes, such as the heme group of c-cytochrome. In this regard, Vasylevskyi et al. demonstrated the electron transfer (ET) potential of Fe2+/heme of c-cytochrome for the reduction of Ag+ to AgNPs in peptide [4], while the Fe2+/heme is oxidized to Fe3+/heme. This is a further testament to the electron interplay between the two iron oxidation states, which have been implicated in diverse biochemical processes in cells.

From an environmental perspective, Fe is an important resource for a vibrant industrial sector (automobile, manufacturing, construction, etc); consequently, its enormous use in the environment has led to grievous pollution of the common environmental compartments (water and soil). Fe2+, for instance, is known to induce the growth of Fe-dependent bacteria and consequently lead to lake eutrophication [5]. This manifests in a foul odor and complete destruction of water aesthetics. Consequently, the maximum permissible level of Fe2+ and Fe3+ in drinking water is pegged at 0.3 mg/L (5.4 μM), by the World Health Organization (WHO) [6].

Reported detection methods for Fe2+ include flame atomic absorption spectrometry(FAAS) [7], chromatography [8], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, ICP-MS [10]. Others include, detection methods working on surface enhanced resonance Raman scattering, (SERRS) principle [6], fluorescence-based assays [11], colorimetry [12], and electrochemistry [13]. While these methods showed good detection efficacies, inherent limitations in the detection principles or in the bulkiness of the equipment used (chromatography, ICP-MS, and AAS) call for a facile approach with reliable sensitivity and selectivity.

Metal nanoparticle (MNP)-based analytical detection of wide-ranging analytes has revolutionized research in analytical chemistry. Their optical property in an aqueous environment is sensitive to many factors (temperature, surrounding media in which they are embedded, pH, ionic strength, precursor materials applied for their synthesis, etc.) [14]. They possess an easily tunable surface, which permits stable and reliable surface functionalization with ligands and biological recognition molecules (aptamers, antibodies, affibodies, and DNA) [15]. As a result, MNP-based assays have been applied in ascorbic acid detection [16], hydrogen peroxide detection [17], dopamine sensing [18] etc. Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are one of the most common MNPs used in the analytical detection or sensing of different analytes. Its sparkling yellowish color with an absorption maximum between 390 and 450 nm makes it a reliable substrate for naked-eye enabled detection, which is equally correlated to changes in the absorption spectra, in response to the addition of diverse analytes of interest. Among the various synthesis methods for MNPs, (physical, chemical, and biological), bio-based methods have become very popular over the past few decades, owing to their perceived environmental friendliness. To promote the concept of environmental sustainability, the use of nature-sourced materials has been adduced as a pragmatic approach to lessening the effect of toxic chemicals on the planet. Biopolymer-mediated synthesis of MNPs holds great promise in nanotechnology, owing to their huge deposits in nature.

Biopolymers, in simple terms, are naturally available macromolecules whosesources originate from animals, plants, and microorganisms [19]. Pullulan (PUL) is one of the commonly available microorganism-based polysaccharide biopolymers from the fungus Aureobasidium pullulans. It is highly hydrophilic, biodegradable, biocompatible, readily available, cheap, and has good water-soluble properties. Its good film forming property has been well exploited for the fabrication of durable and responsive packaging films for food shelf-life extension [20,21]. Owing to the presence of multitudinous hydroxyl groups in the pullulan structure, its capacity to act as a reductant of metal salts for MNP fabrication has been demonstrated [22–24].

Fe2+role as a reducing agent has garnered scientific interest recently. For example, Djafari et al. applied FeSO4 as the only reductant for the synthesis of colloidal AuNPs, further stabilized by polystyrenesulfonate (PSS) [25]. Similarly, Liu et al. synthesized Au/Fe3O4 hybrid through a room temperature based redox reaction between FeSO4 and Au3+ salt [26], while a colorimetric assay has been reported for the detection of Fe2+ using polymethylacrylic acid-directed silver nanocluster synthesis [27].

Motivated by the above, herein we design the synthesis of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ at ambient temperature under excess AgNO3 salt in the synthesis reaction solution, using PUL as the only reductant/stabilizing agent. Our interest here is to synthesize a colloidal AgNPs-based nanoprobe containing both AgNPs, or Ag0, and excess or unreduced silver ions (Ag+) from the injected AgNO3, using biocompatible and stable polymeric chains of pullulan as the matrix for the immobilization of silver ions (Ag0 and Ag+). The choice of PUL is based on its reductive capacity (arising from its high hydroxyl group content), availability, cost effectiveness, and good aqueous stability. Thus, the synthesized Ag0-PUL-Ag+ at optimal conditions contained a fair composition of Ag0 and Ag+, fixed in the PUL matrix. We envisioned that the addition of Fe2+ to Ag0-PUL-Ag+ nanoprobe would activate a redox reaction between Fe2+ and Ag+, supported by buffer and an appropriate pH, so that the inherent Ag+ in Ag0-PUL-Ag+ may be reduced to Ag0 to form more AgNPs with concomitant color changes and absorption spectra enhancement. The conceptualized hypothesis was investigated with a view to developing a sensitive and selective colorimetric assay for Fe2+ with detection capacity in different real samples. Moreover, the detection strategy proposed would confer high selectivity towards Fe2+ detection in comparison with other reported probes, which mostly suffer from the inability to distinguish between Fe2+ and Fe3+ states. Further, the nanoprobe obviated the use of environmentally noxious materials for its synthesis and thus conformed to the green chemistry concept or while showcasing the strength of PUL polymeric microstructure in directing MNPs synthesis and stabilization for analytical detection/sensing of very important metal ions.

Pullulan (PUL) of molecular weight 50–70 kDa was procured from TCI Japan. AgNO3 (Silver nitrate), 99.8%, was from RCI Labscan Thailand. FeSO4.7H2O (Iron (II) sulfate heptahydrate) and LiCl (Lithium chloride) were from Merck, Germany. NaNO3 (Sodium nitrate) and KNO3 (Potassium nitrate) from Spectrum Chem Corp. USA, Pb(NO3)2 (Lead (II) nitrate), and CdCl2.2.5H2O (Cadmium chloride hemipentahydrate) was from Sigma Aldrich, Singapore. Ca(NO3)2.4H2O (Calcium nitrate tetrahydrate), CoCl2.6H2O (Cobalt (II) chloride hexahydrate), Cr(NO3)3 (Chromium (III) nitrate), FeCl3 (Iron (III) chloride), MgCl2.6H2O (Magnesium chloride hexahydrate), and MnCl2.4H2O (Manganese (II) chloride tetrahydrate) were from QReC (New Zealand). ZnSO4.7H2O (Zinc sulfate heptahydrate), CuSO4.5H2O (Copper sulphate pentahydrate), BaCl2 (Barium chloride), NiCl2.6H2O (Nickel chloride hexahydrate), Al(NO3)2.9H2O (Aluminum nitrate nonahydrate) is all from Ajax Finechem Ltd. (Australia). Ultra-pure water from the Millipore purification system was used in the preparation and synthesis of nanoparticles and the metal ion detection assays.

Synthesis success was monitored on an Agilent 8453 UV-vis spectrophotometer, and photo images were collected using a mobile phone. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of the sample was observed on JEM 2010 equipment from JEOL, Japan, while prepared dried film samples were profiled using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) from APREO, FEI. The FESEM set-up was linked to an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) machine (X-Max 80, from Oxford Instruments, United Kingdom). The X-ray diffractogram spectra (XRD) were collected on an Empyrean machine from 5 to 80°. The incident beam was a CuKα type with a scan speed of 70.2 s, and a wavelength of 0.154 nm. The thermal degradation profile was obtained from a TGA 8000 (Perkin Elmer, USA). A small mass of the sample was exposed to an incremental temperature change from 50 to 800 °C at an increment of 10 °C/min in a nitrogen environment. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was from the Kratos AXIS Ultra XPS spectrometer from Shimadzu, Japan. The sample was prepared and dropped on the surface of a silicon wafer before being inserted into the instrument. Functional group elucidation was acquired using an ATR-FTIR system (Nicolet IS50, Thero-Fisher) and a Raman force spectrometer from nanophoton for Raman spectra information. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta-potential were obtained from the same equipment, malvern zetasizer nano. Appropriately diluted aqueous solutions of the tested samples were poured into a capillary cell and inserted in the sample compartment, and the equipment was adjusted to run (n = 100) for each analysis and was further run-in triplicate.

Prior to the synthesis, a 6% PUL solution was prepared in distilled water by dissolving the appropriate mass of the polymer in distilled water under stirring. Afterward, the aqueous solution was raised to 60 °C and maintained for 1 h to facilitate the complete dissolution of PUL. The heat source was disconnected, and the solution was stored in the refrigerator before use. This is to allow the complete dissolution of pullulan in water.

For Ag0-PUL-Ag+ synthesis, 40 mL of a 6% PUL solution was measured into a beaker under stirring. The beaker was covered with aluminum foil in order to prevent photooxidation effect. Then, 5.0 mL of AgNO3 solution at 30 mM was added, followed by, 0.25 mL of 0.1 M NaOH solution, which was then made up to 46 mL with water. The mixture was subjected to regular heating at 50 °C (the optimized temperature) and was maintained for 1h. The heating was stopped, and the solution was stirred to room temperature, then stored in an amber-colored reagent bottle at 4 °C before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical detection of Fe2+ using Ag0-PUL-Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: For a 5 mL detection tube, 160 μL of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ colloidal solution was added, then 840 μL of acetate buffer, having a concentration of 0.1 M and pH 5.0 (optimized pH), was injected, and then 1 mL of Fe2+ solution of different concentrations was added to furnish Fe2+ final concentrations from 0.00 to 150.0 μM, for a 2.0 mL total reaction volume. The reaction solution was mixed on a vortex mixer and allowed to react or incubate at room temperature (RT) for 30 min. The UV–Vis absorption spectra and the photo images were then recorded accordingly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ towards Fe2+ detection in tap water and ferrous fumarate tablet
ce:section.ce:para:
The practical application of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ as an optical probe for Fe2+ was demonstrated using our laboratory tap water at the Department of Chemistry, Chulalongkorn University, Thailand. The tap water was open to flow for a few minutes before the sample was collected. The water sample was then boiled to remove residual chlorine, and a 0.22-μm syringe filter was applied to remove any particles. Fe2+ detection was performed using the standard addition method by charging the processed water and the sample of Fe2+ standard-charged at different concentrations on the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ probe. The accuracy, expressed as percent recovery, is estimated using the below equation,
Recovery = (C1 – C0)/C2 ˟ 100%,
Abbreviation is explained as, C1 = spiked sample concentration, C0 = unspiked sample concentration, and C2 is the concentration of standard added.

Fe2+ detection in ferrous fumarate tablets was carried out as follows; ferrous fumarate was procured from a pharmacy shop in Bangkok, Thailand. The weight of one tablet was noted, then five tablets were ground properly in a porcelain mortar, and the equivalent of one tablet was weighed and dissolved in 250 mL of ultra-pure water under stirring at RT. The sample was filtered and passed through a 0.22-μm filter. 1 mL of the sample was made up to 10 mL with ultra-pure water for the unspiked sample, while the same volume was spiked with different concentrations of FeSO4.7H2O to complete 10 mL for the spiked sample solution. The prepared sample solution was injected on the PUL-AgNPs probe, similar to section 2.4. The UV–Vis absorption spectra and the associated Photographic images were obtained after 30 min, while the Fe2+ detection in tablets, using the titration method between FeSO4.7H2O and potassium permanganate (VII) KMnO4, (a self-indicating reaction), was applied for method validation.

The synthesis protocol adopted for the fabrication of pullulan (PUL) reduced and stabilized AgNPs, denoted as Ag0-PUL-Ag+, is shown in Fig. 1 . As depicted, the perfect aqueous dispersity of PUL in water was capitalized on to facilitate the successful synthesis of Ag0-PUL-Ag+. In fact, previous works have shown that PUL-mediated reduction of metal ions towards MNP synthesis at ambient conditions is quite challenging. For instance, Kanmani et al. [23] synthesized stable PUL-AgNPs through heating at 121 °C for 15 min using a 15% PUL solution. Coseri et al. applied the popular TEMPO oxidation method to native pullulan to improve its reductive efficiency and then realized 6-carboxypullulan for PUL-AgNPs at room temperature over 6 h [28]. Motivated by the sodium hydroxide (NaOH) supplemented strategy adopted by Laksee et al. [24], here, for the synthesis of our Ag0-PUL-Ag+ probe, we used NaOH solution to fasten the synthesis success. It is important to state that the addition of NaOH would also facilitate the breakdown of PUL polymer backbones into smaller reductive species through alkali hydrolysis, while the distal hydroxyl group of PUL (Fig. 1) is oxidized to carboxylic groups, which provides surface stabilization for the formed Ag0-PUL-Ag+. Thus, NaOH could catalyze the reaction through the generation of more reductive functionalities to supply electrons for the eventual reduction of AgNO3 to its nanoparticles, or Ag0. The formation of a yellowish colloidal solution with a UV–Vis absorption maximum at 415 nm within 1 h is a confirmation of the successful formation of Ag0-PUL-Ag+. Colloidal MNP synthesis follows three common steps, which include metal ion reduction/breakdown (small nuclei formation), then the particles are clustered together (cluster formation), and particle growth and maturation [29]. The color generation and absorption maximum development are typical of MNPs, particularly Ag, Cu, Au, etc. This property is called the surface plasmon resonance, where MNP surface conduction electrons are set into excitation/oscillation after electromagnetic light interaction [30]. From the outset, the major aim of the synthesis is to generate a cornucopia of residual Ag+ ions by using excess AgNO3, which can be immobilized in the PUL matrix owing to the advantageous aqueous stability of PUL biopolymer. As shown in Fig. 1, after NaOH injection and 1 h of heating at 50 °C, Ag0-PUL-Ag+ is realized, containing a fair ratio of Ag0/Ag+. That is, under the current synthesis condition, PUL is incapable of reducing all the excess AgNO3 injected, so yellow Ag0 is formed, containing high loads of Ag+, all in the PUL matrix. We have hypothesized that with the high load of Ag+ in our Ag0-PUL-Ag+ probe, the addition of Fe2+ in the form of FeSO4.7H2O will set up a redox reaction between Ag+ and Fe2+, aided by an appropriate pH and buffer.

While keeping the concentrations of AgNO3, PUL, and NaOH constant in the reaction pot of 46 mL constant, the effect of temperature variation on PUL synthesis was investigated. Fig. 2 (a) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ realized at different temperatures (30, 50,70, and 90 °C) after appropriate dilutions of the colloidal solution. As can be seen, the absorption spectra increased as the temperature of the reaction pot increased, owing to the enhancement of PUL reductive capacity, which will permit more AgNO3 to be reduced to AgNPs. The yellowish color of AgNPs can be observed (Fig. 2(a) inset), which attests to the effectiveness of the synthesis protocol. Fig. 2(b) shows a table summarizing the properties of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ synthesized at different temperatures. The absorbance maximum of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ decreased as the temperature increased from 30 to 90 °C, which may be explained by proposing that increased temperature will enhance the reductive strength of PUL, towards the reduction of more AgNO3 in solution, with concomitant formation of AgNPs with better or smaller particles sizes. In fact, the position of the UV–Vis maximum of AgNPs has been correlated with particles sizes [31], hence the progressive decrement in the absorbance maximum observed in this work may be interpreted to mean improved or reduced particle sizes formation. The absorbance ratios (A415/A650) could provide more information on the colloidal Ag0-PUL-Ag+ formed at different temperatures. The A415 represents the absorbance of well dispersed AgNPs, while the A650 denotes the absorbance of aggregated AgNPs. As shown in the table, the ratio increased progressively as the synthesis temperature increased, which further confirmed the fact that better nanoparticle distribution is formed at higher temperatures. Notably also, synthesis temperature increment promoted a progressive increase in the absorbance intensity, which is attributed to temperature induced increased reduction of silver salt to its nanoparticles through effective activation of multitudinous hydroxyl groups of PUL. The size of the nanoparticles obtained from the dynamic light scattering (DLS) equipment and the zeta potential (which reveals particle charges and stability) show that the particle sizes reduced and the zeta potential increased as the synthesis temperature increased. From the foregoing, it can be summarized that high temperatures favor the formation of AgNPs with better properties owing to the increased reduction of AgNO3 to AgNPs, while moderate temperatures will permit more residual AgNO3 in the formed AgNPs, which is of interest in this work. After initial profiling of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ synthesized at different temperatures, it was found that Ag0-PUL-Ag+ realized at 50 °C gave an impressive response to Fe2+, and was selected as the optimal synthesis condition, and was further fully characterized with several analytical instruments.

The stability of the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ at 50 °C was monitored over a time frame of eleven weeks, as presented in Fig. S1. The UV–Vis spectrum remained unchanged, with a sharp peak at 415 nm and without any noticeable peak characteristic of aggregated AgNPs. This shows the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ is very stable when properly stored (refrigerator storage) and can be used for sensing over a long time. This stability needs to be clarified on the basis of strong intermolecular interactions between the pullulan polymeric chains and the reduced silver ions (Ag0 and Ag+). It is also worth stating that there is no further reduction of unreduced Ag+ in the formed Ag0-PUL-Ag+ since refrigeration storage cannot promote any reduction of inherent ions.

Using different analytical equipment, information on the microstructural properties of PUL biopolymer film and Ag0-PUL-Ag+ was acquired. As presented in Fig. 3 . The TEM images of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ (Fig. 3(a)) show spherical and well dispersed particles, with an average particle size distribution between 20 and 30 nm (Fig. 3(b)). This shows the synthesis condition generates AgNPs with close sizes. Further information on the particle images was probed using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM). The PUL aqueous solution, which was freeze dried to a film, shows smooth surfaces (Fig. 3(c)), while Ag0-PUL-Ag+ (Fig. 3(d)) revealed the distribution of small-sized AgNPs (whitish spots) in the PUL matrix.

Moreover, the functional group interplay between PUL and Ag0-PUL-Ag+ is displayed in the ATR-FTIR result (Fig. 3(e)). The PUL spectrum (Fig. 3(e) blue line) shows peaks at 3296 (OH stretching vibration of PUL multitudinous hydroxyl groups), 2922 (C–H) stretching vibration, 1640 (O–C–O stretching), 1350 (C–O–H bending band), and 1145 (C–O–C stretching band), while the peaks are at 991 (α(1,4)) and 750 cm-1 (α(1,6)) glycosidic linkages in the PUL molecule [32]. For the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ spectrum (Fig. 3(e) red line), major peaks identified in PUL are all present, along with a minor shift, which confirms the interaction of PUL functional groups in realizing Ag0-PUL-Ag+ while also capping or stabilizing the nanoparticles. Moreover, the peaks reduction in the regions marked A and B in the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ probe in comparison to the pristine PUL also shows the presence of intermolecular interaction between AgNPs and the functional groups of PUL.

Raman spectroscopy provides another important instrumental setup to profile the changes in biopolymer functional groups when applied in nanofabrication. Fig. 3(f) shows the Raman spectrum of PUL and Ag0-PUL-Ag+. As shown, the Raman peaks of PUL (Fig. 3(f) blue line) include 857.8 (C–O–C asymmetrical vibrational band), 941.9 (C–O–C stretching vibrational band), 1135.5 (C–C bending vibration), 1342.3 (O–H bending vibration), 1460.6 (bending vibration of C–H), 2922.7 (C–H stretching), and 3460.5 cm-1 (O–H stretching of PUL hydroxyl moiety) [33]. However, in the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ spectrum (Fig. 3(f) red line), most of the identified peaks of PUL have either diminished or disappeared, owing to the application of PUL as the reducing and capping agent of the synthesized Ag0-PUL-Ag+. The significant reduction in the pristine PUL Raman peaks at 2922.7 and 3460.5 cm-1 confirmed the conversion of the hydroxyl groups of PUL to carboxylate ions, resulting from PUL polymer chain interactions with AgNPs. As noted, comparison between the results from FTIR and Raman shows that in the FTIR spectra of PUL and Ag0-PUL-Ag+, stark functional group alterations were not observed, unlike the result from Raman. This may be due to the instrument sensitivitiy; moreover, the Raman baseline spectra were subtracted from the acquired spectra. Exhilaratingly, both results confirm the functional group interplay between PUL and silver ions fixed in the polymer chains.
Further information on PUL and Ag0-PUL-Ag+ structural and morphological changes was acquired and presented in Fig. 4 .

The X-ray diffractogram (XRD) result is presented in Fig. 4 (a). PUL polymer film (Fig. 4(a) blue line) shows sharp peaks at 18.3°, which is attributed to the amorphous nature of PUL polymer. However, for the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ (Fig. 4(a) red line), peaks at 19.2 and 38.5° were observable. The peaks at 19.2° are from PUL, which obviously shifted in comparison to PUL, attesting to the role of PUL in modulating Ag0-PUL-Ag+ synthesis. The peak at 38.5° is from the (111) crystalline peaks of face-centered cubic (fcc) AgNPs [34]. Other characteristic peaks of (fcc) AgNPs crystallinity were not revealed, which may be due to the low concentration of AgNPs in the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ for the equipment to detect. The peak shift of PUL from 18.3° (2θ degree) to 19.2° in Ag0-PUL-Ag+, is an indication of pullulan polymer chain alignment alterations, i.e. d-spacing decrement [35], calculated from Bragg's equation (d = nλ/2Sinθ). As shown in Fig. 4(a) inset, the inter-chain spacing in the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ probe decreases owing to the chain compactness induced by the interaction of AgNPs with PUL. This result is in strong agreement with the observations noted in ATR-FTIR and Raman.

The energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) result for Ag0-PUL-Ag+is presented in Fig. 4(b). Sharp peaks of Carbon (C 65.7%), oxygen (O 32.1%), and silver (Ag 2.3%) and the percent elemental composition (Fig. 4(b) inset) confirmed the presence of biopolymers and AgNPs. The elemental mapping of the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ film is displayed in Fig. S2. The surface profile shows AgNP dispersion inside the PUL matrix, similar to the FESEM result, while the presence of C, O, and Ag elements was also confirmed.

The thermal or heat stability of PUL film and Ag0-PUL-Ag+ was investigated through TGA and DTG, Fig. 4 (c and d). Three prominent temperature degradation stages can be identified in the TGA profile (Fig. 4(c)). These stages are presented in Table S1. For the PUL film (Fig. 4(c) blue line), the three stages occurred at (28.4–260.3 °C) stage I, (260.3–451.7 °C) stage II, and (451.7–800 °C) stage III, with the onset of degradation beginning at 28.4 °C. The ash content after the complete degradation of the polymer was 0.01%. For the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ film (Fig. 4(c) red line), the onset of degradation was at 26.8 °C, with the three stages set at (26.8–232.3 °C) stage I, (232.3–493.7 °C) stage II, and (493.7–800 °C) stage III. The percentage ash for the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ was 10.35. The three degradation stages are generally assigned to water moisture removal (stage I), polymer-ordered network rupturing (stage II), and polymer complete carbonization stage or ash stage (stage III) [36]. The high ash content of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ confirmed the high thermal stability of AgNPs in the PUL matrix and significantly improved the TGA of the resulting Ag0-PUL-Ag+ in comparison with pristine PUL film. This is likely so, as Ag0 and Ag+ are firmly enveloped in the PUL matrix after reaction with AgNO3. The DTG of PUL and Ag0-PUL-Ag+ presented in Fig. 4(d) further confirmed the various stages identified in TGA. It also showed that most thermal degradation of PUL and Ag0-PUL-Ag+ occurred around 300 °C, hence the presence of a sharp peak (Fig. 4(d), blue and red lines).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy is another very vital instrument used for nanomaterial characterization. It can provide information about the composition of the available elements present in a material and the various oxidation states of the identified elements. This may be helpful to investigate the successful reduction of metal salts to their various nanoparticles and the presence of other higher oxidation states of the metal. The full scan survey of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ film is shown in Fig. 4(e). Peaks of C, O, and Ag are displayed with the percent composition. The high-resolution deconvoluted spectra of the various elements are presented in Fig. 4(f–h). The C 1s deconvoluted spectra in Fig. 4(f) revealed peaks at 284.7 eV (C–C), 286.1 eV (C–O), 287.1 eV (C=O), 287.8 eV (C–O–C) and 289.9 eV (O=C–O) [37]. These peaks reveal the different carbon environments present in the PUL biopolymer’s interaction with the AgNPs. The Ag 3d deconvoluted spectra in Fig. 4(g) revealed the presence of peaks at 367.6 eV, which corresponded to (Ag+ 3d5/2) and the presence of a sharp peak at 373.6 peak for (Ag+ 3d3/2) while other peaks at 368.4 eV (Ag0 3d5/2) and 374.4 eV (Ag0 3d3/2), were all identified, similar to previous reports [38,39]. This result importantly validated the presence of both Ag+ and Ag0 in the synthesized Ag0-PUL-Ag+. Thus, the conceptualization and design of the present Ag0-PUL-Ag+ using moderate temperature and excessive AgNO3 could engender Ag0-PUL-Ag+ containing completely reduced Ag0 and un-reduced Ag+ all in a PUL matrix. This synthesis is like the work reported by Teerasong et al. where the synthesis of polyvinyl alcohol reduced and stabilized AgNPs for antioxidant assays, using gallic acid as a proof of concept, [][40]. Further processing of the Ag0 and Ag+ peaks shows a ratio of 60:40. That is, the fabricated Ag0-PUL-Ag+ probe contains more Ag0 than Ag+, though with high loads of Ag+. Furthermore, the O 1s deconvoluted spectra in Fig. 4(h) show peaks at 531.6 eV that can be assigned to the (C–O–H) bond, 532.4 eV (C–O–H…O..) and the 532.6 eV peak that can be given to the (C–O–C) of oxygen linked to hydrogen and carbon atoms. From the detailed instrumental characterization employed to profile PUL and Ag0-PUL-Ag+, the role of PUL as a stabilizing and reducing agent towards Ag0-PUL-Ag+ synthesis has been confirmed. Moreover, using excess AgNO3 salts and a moderate temperature of 50 °C, Ag0-PUL-Ag+ furnished nanoparticles containing a fair share of Ag0 and Ag+, which can initiate a redox reaction with Fe2+ when injected, can realize a sensitive and specific colorimetric assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Mechanism of Fe
2+
detection strategy based on Ag0-PUL-Ag+
ce:section.ce:para:
The underlining detection mechanism of the Ag0-PUL-Ag+colorimetric nanoprobe for Fe2+ is proposed and explained to be based on Fe2+ and Ag+ redox interactions. As explained supra from the synthesis steps, it has been confirmed that under the present synthesis of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ at the optimum conditions, PUL is incapable of effecting a complete reduction of the excess AgNO3 used for the synthesis. Thus, while AgNPs are formed, the colloidal solution of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ is replete with unreduced Ag+ ions. As proved by the XPS result, Ag0/Ag+ is 60:40. As shown in Fig. 5 (a) the fabricated Ag0-PUL-Ag+ is believed to have a fair share of Ag0 and Ag+, all intertwined in the polymeric structure of PUL through intermolecular interactions. Under the influence of buffer and Fe2+, this ordered arrangement between PUL and Ag0/Ag+ is disrupted. Consequently, a redox reaction is activated between Ag+ and Fe2+, which results in Ag+ being reduced to Ag0 or more AgNPs, which are further deposited on the initially present AgNPs in the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ probe. As a result, the color of the initially faint yellow Ag0-PUL-Ag+ changes to a deeper yellow, orange, and brownish yellow over 30 min. This also manifests in absorption spectra enhancement. Hypothetically representing Ag0-PUL-Ag+ as given in Fig. 5(a), with Ag0 and Ag+ well immobilized in PUL matrix, then under acetate buffer pH 5.0 and Fe2+, we hypothesized that the redox reaction between Ag+ and Fe2+ (Fig. 5(a) stage I) will engender more AgNP formation, which will further be deposited on the initially formed Ag0 fixed in the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ probe (Fig. 5(a) stage II). This will lead to color perturbations and absorption spectrum enhancement. This proposition can be supported by the electrochemical series. The standard electrode potential (SEP) of Fe2+/Fe3+ is 0.77 V, while that Ag+/Ag0 is 0.80 V [41]. As generally known from the series, metals with higher SEP values will serve as oxidizing agents in comparison with those with lower SEP values. From the foregoing, for the redox reaction between Ag+ and Fe2+, Ag+ will serve as the oxidizing agent. Thus, Fe2+ is oxidized to Fe3+, while Ag+ is reduced simultaneously to Ag0. The cell potential (E0 cell) of the above Ag+ and Fe2+ redox reactions is given as,

The Gibb's free energy (ΔG0), of the reaction, which is another thermodynamic term that reflects the feasibility of a redox reaction, can be estimated.

n = number of electrons transferred in the reaction, F = Faraday's constant, or 96,485 C/mol, and E0 cell is the cell potential. Thus, the ΔG0 of the redox reaction between Ag+ and Fe2+ is −2895J. The negative value is a confirmation of the reaction’s spontaneity or feasibility.

The particle TEM images of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ in the absence of 0.0 μM Fe2+ and at 100.0 μM Fe2+ injection are shown in Fig. 5(b). The images from the TEM accordingly revealed highly dispersed and spherical nanoparticles in the absence (Fig. 5(b) image) and presence of 100.0 μM Fe2+ (Fig. 5(b) b image), which further validated that the particles were not degraded or aggregated, consequent upon Fe2+ injection. The hydrodynamic diameter from DLS as presented shows Ag0-PUL-Ag+ in the absence of Fe2+ possessed a value of 96.6 ± 2.3 nm (Fig. 5(b) c image), while a slight particle increment to 117.4 ± 3.4 nm (Fig. 5(b) d image) was observed after 100.0 μM of Fe2+ was injected. This particle increment may be attributed to Fe2+-mediated reduction of Ag+ to Ag0 or AgNPs, which are deposited on the initial Ag0-PUL-Ag+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ as a colorimetric probe for Fe2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: MNP-based optical probe analytical sensing is affected by many factors. Thus, for optimum response to be achieved, various factors affecting the detection assay must be optimized. The optimization conditions and the detailed explanations are contained in the supplementary materials (Figs. S3–S5). Accordingly, acetate buffer 0.1 M of pH 5.0, subjected to an incubation time of 30 min, was adopted as the optimized conditions for reproducible Fe2+ detection using the synthesized Ag0-PUL-Ag+ as a colorimetric nanoprobe.

The prospect of exploring Ag0-PUL-Ag+ as an optical probe for Fe2+ in solution was first tested. As depicted in Fig. 6 (a), Ag0-PUL-Ag+ shows a sharp absorption maximum at 415 nm (Fig. 6(a), orange line), with characteristic yellowish color (Fig. 6(a), label an inset). With the addition of acetate buffer, there is a slight shift in the absorption spectrum (Fig. 6(a), blue line), owing to the change in dielectric of the medium in which the nanoparticles were dispersed. The localized surface plasmon resonance phenomenon of MNPs is significantly dependent on the refractive and dielectric properties of the embedding environment [42]. The color of the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ in buffer solution is still yellowish (Fig. 6(a) label b inset). In fact, this aqueous solution is stable for over 5 days, even at room temperature. This shows the buffer concentration on its own does not affect the aqueous stability of Ag0-PUL-Ag+. Fig. 6(a) The red line shows the absorbance of Fe2+ tested at the highest concentration of 150.0 μM and buffer, showing that the buffer does not interact with the Fe2+ ion added. The colorless image (Fig. 6(a) label c inset) further validated that no form of color development occurred from the reaction of buffer and Fe2+. Excitingly, the addition of Ag0-PUL-Ag+, buffer pH 5.0, and Fe 2+, vortex mixed briefly, and left to incubate for 30 min, led to a big absorption spectra enhancement (Fig. 6(a) yellow line) with deep yellowish brown color development (Fig. 6(a) label d inset). This shows the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ holds practicable application for Fe2+ detection in positive confirmation of this work hypothesis. The importance of excess Ag+ in the fabricated AgNPs was emphasized through control experiments using PUL-stabilized AgNPs and NaBH4 as the reducing agent. NaBH4 is an important and popular chemical reductant in nanomaterial synthesis, which oftentimes furnishes colloidal nanoparticles with small sizes, good dispersity, and stability [43,44]. Consequently, PUL-AgNPs realized through the NaBH4-based method are believed to be devoid of any residual Ag+ ion, owing to their high reductive efficiency. This was tested under acetate buffer supplementation for Fe2+ detection, as depicted in Fig. S6, while maintaining the same buffer choice (acetate buffer) at pH 5.0 and 0.1 M concentration as used for the proposed assay. Accordingly, PUL-AgNPs in water show maximum absorption at 399 nm (Fig. S6 (a)), with a light-yellow color (Fig. S6 (a) inset). The addition of acetate buffer had no noticeable impact on the absorption spectrum (Fig. S6 (b)) or the resulting visual image (Fig. S6 (b) inset). The addition of Fe2+ at 150 μM, did not induce absorption spectrum increment (Fig. S6 (c)) or color change (Fig. S6 (c) inset), while a significant increment in Fe2+ at 10,000 μM, failed to elicit significant absorption spectrum increment (Fig. S6 (d)) or distinct color perturbation (Fig. S6 (d) inset). This shows that the synthesis strategy is key to the successful application of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ as an optical probe for Fe2+ detection.

The sensitivity of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ was investigated afterward, by charging different concentrations of Fe2+ using an acetate buffer of pH 5.0. Fe2+ final concentrations in a 2 mL reaction pot varied from 1.0 to 150.0 μM. As can be clearly observed with the naked eye (Fig. 6(b)), the addition of Fe2+ to the stable and yellowish color Ag0-PUL-Ag+ led to progressive color perturbations from light yellow to yellow, orange, and brownish yellow. The absorption spectra of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ under incremental addition of Fe2+ show consistent spectra enhancement (Fig. 6(c)). Accordingly, the absorption maximum at 411 nm increased in a regular fashion, which also emphasized the effectiveness of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ to serve as a detection probe for Fe2+ as an analyte. The change in absorbance at 411 nm (ΔA@411), which is the difference between the absorbance of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ in the presence of various concentrations of Fe2+ (AFe2+) and in the absence of Fe2+ (Ao), i.e., (ΔA@411 = AFe2+ - Ao), plotted against the full concentration range of Fe2+, is presented in Fig. 6(d). As shown, the ratio increased regularly, in support of the naked-eye observation. The plot, when interpreted in detail, shows two linear plots Fig. 6(e), for the low concentration range (1.0–20.0 μM), which can be slotted into the equation ΔA@411 = 0.0112[Fe2+] + 0.0374, with an R2 value to 0.9927. The linearity plot for the high concentration range, 20.0–125.0 μM, is shown in Fig. 6(f), according to the equation, ΔA@411 = 0.0058[Fe2+] + 0.1387, with R2 value to 0.9962. The limit of detection (LOD) of the assay was computed from the low calibration plot using the equation (3.3 ˟ Sa/b)/slope, where Sa/b represents the standard deviation of the calibration curve regression and slope is obtained from the calibration curve. The LOD value therefore calculated was 0.15 μM, which is far below the acceptable limit of 5.4 μM, from the WHO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ with other reported works for Fe2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical features of the Ag0-PUL-Ag+detection assay for Fe2+ were compared with previous works, as presented in Table 1 . As can be observed, our Ag0-PUL-Ag+ demonstrated better or comparable analytical features in relation to other works. The electrochemical system, as can be seen, can detect to a very low detection limit [45]. However, the set-up requires careful design and fabrication of special electrodes, which may restrict its wider range of applications. However, colorimetric assays present simplicity in that the response can be viewed with the naked eyes, and the read-out equipment (UV–Vis Spectrophotometer) is about the most readily available analytical equipment, even in an economically challenged environment. Methods based on fluorimetry on the other hand may be limited by the cost of fluorimeters and the possibility of auto-fluorescence of detection probe even in the absence of the analyte. Hence, the possibility of false positives is prevalent, which also places a challenge on the method's robustness and applicability. Moreover, the fluorescence-based detection strategy showed the worst selectivity for single iron oxidation state detection [14,45,47–49,51]. This must have resulted from the similar binding properties of Fe2+ and Fe3+ with specially designed fluorophores. It is worth noting that, while most of the previous works deliberately avoided testing the capacity of their method to respond singly to Fe2+ over Fe3+, our work is highly selective towards Fe2+ only, even at a significantly higher concentration of Fe3+. This is well attributed to the careful design of the detection system to explore the reduction capacity of Fe2+ in a redox reaction with Ag+. However, since the analytical response of Fe2+ is based on absorption spectra enhancement, this method may suffer from possible interference from strongly reducing metabolites such as ascorbic acid (tested, result not shown). The practical real samples adopted for now do not contain any interfering reducing metabolites. Hence, the method is safe for Fe2+ quality assurance investigation and monitoring in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Precision and selectivity of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ towards Fe2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Precision and selectivity are two major features expected of a MNP-based nanosensor, designed for real samples utilitarian purposes. The precision of a detection probe is expected to fulfill repeatability and reproducibility expressed as coefficient of variation (CV) or, commonly, a, relative standard deviation (RSD%). Repeatability or intra-day precision is the closeness of replicate measurements, while reproducibility could be envisioned asthe ability of a detection response to follow closely without significant deviations. Fig. 7 (a), shows the UV–Vis profile of ten replicate Fe2+ concentrations at 20.0 μM, does not show any major differences, which is supported by the photo images (Fig. 7(a) inset), while the UV–Vis absorption spectra of Fe2+ concentrations at 100.0 μM (Fig. 7(b)) and the photo images (Fig. 7(b) inset) confirm a non-variable analytical response. The blank sample photo is also displayed to compare the responses briefly. For reproducibility or inter-day precision, the tested Fe2+ concentrations at 20.0 and 100.0 μM were charged on the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ over three consecutive days at (n = 5) on each day. The calculated RSD obtained was (1.5 & 2.4) % for the intra-day and (3.2 & 3.8) % for inter-day precision, respectively, at the tested concentrations.

The selectivity of the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ probe in the presence of wide-ranging possible interfering cations was assessed by charging the tested metal salts on Ag0-PUL-Ag+ at optimal detection conditions for Fe2+. The final concentration of the metal ions was fixed at 300.0 μM, while Fe2+ was fixed at 60.0 μM. Fig. 7(c) shows the photographs of the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ probe after the addition of several metal ions. As demonstrated, only the addition of Fe2+, even at a five-times lesser concentration, could engender significant color changes (orange), while other metal ions were all yellowish or faded yellow. The UV–Vis absorption spectra in Fig. 7(d) validated that only Fe2+ could impart an absorbance enhancement, owing to its redox reaction with the cornucopia of Ag+ in our carefully fabricated Ag0-PUL-Ag+. The analytical response (ΔA@411) plotted against various metal ions (Fig. 7(e)) shows that Fe2+ alone generated a high response in comparison to other metal ions. The ability of the probe to respond to Fe2+ in a simulated environment of Fe2+ mixed with each single metal ion (Fe2+ = 60.0 and metal ions = 300.0 μM), is displayed in Fig. 7(f). The response of Fe2+ was in no way affected by the addition of other metal ions, even at higher concentrations; that is, the responses of Fe 2+ alone and Fe2+ mixed with metal ions (potential interfering species) did not show notable significant differences (p ≥ 0.05). These results show that the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ is highly selective and specific towards Fe2+ detection in solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ towards Fe2+ detection in water and ferrous fumarate tablets
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ for Fe2+ detection in a real sample was carried out using tap water from the Department of Chemistry, Chulalongkorn University, Thailand, and was processed as highlighted in section 2.5. The boiled and filtered water sample was spiked with Fe2+ solution of different concentrations, so that the addition of 1 mL of the spiked sample into a 2 mL reaction pot with Ag0-PUL-Ag+ and buffer would furnish Fe2+ final concen-trations from 1.0 to 150.0 μM, (similar to the concentrations tested in the calibration curve). The response of the unspiked water sample is taken as the concentration of the analyte in the applied sample. This is the basis of the standard addition method of real sample investigation, which is the most acceptable method for real sample determination in analytical method development [53,54]. The UV–Vis absorption spectra and the corresponding photo images were acquired and presented in Fig. S7. Noticeable absorption spectra enhancement (Fig. S7) and color changes (Fig. S7 insets), similar to the trend observed in the calibration curve, were noted, which justifies the practicality of the detection probe, Ag0-PUL-Ag+ to detect Fe2+ in water samples. The plot of ΔA@411 against Fe2+ (1.0–150.0 μM) is shown in Fig. S8. The linearity plot is displayed in Fig. S9, showing strong linearity within 1.0–20.0 μM, ΔA@411 = 0.0107[Fe2+] + 0.0427, R2 value of 0.9938, and 20.0–125.0 μM, ΔA@411 = 0.0068[Fe2+] + 0.1065, R2 value of 0.9941. The response of Fe2+ in a real sample (tap water) and at the calibration curve was compared using a two-way ANOVA to reveal the matrix effect on the detection probe. As can be observed in Fig. S10, there are no observable significant differences (p ≥ 0.05) in the response (slope) of Fe2+ detection within the two media. This shows there is no matrix effect from the real sample used [55], thus Fe2+ accuracy estimation can be computed from either plot. Consequently, the recovery of Fe2+ at three concentrations (10.0, 60.0, and 100.0 μM), is calculated and presented in Table S2. The percent recovery, which is a measure of the method’s accuracy, ranged between 94.8% and 101.2%. This proved the practical potentiality of the detection strategy in real-sample Fe2+ monitoring. From an environmental perspective, the monitoring of Fe2+ in water samples for human use cannot be overemphasized.

Moreover, we also investigated the capacity of the Ag0-PUL-Ag+ probe for Fe2+ detection in ferrous fumarate tablets. Ferrous fumarate, ferrous gluconate, and ferrous succinate are the commonest available drugs for the treatment of Fe2+ deficiency in anemic patients. Hence, Fe2+ detection in these tablets is often investigated as a quality control measure against the reported concentrations from the manufacturers. The tablet was processed as detailed in section 2.5. The sample processed after appropriate dilution is the unspiked sample. Two concentrations of Fe2+, at 20.0 and 80.0 μM, were spiked on the ferrous fumarate sample to prepare a real-sample spiked solution. Fig. 8 shows the absorption spectra of Ag0-PUL-Ag+, the unspiked sample, and the Fe2+ spiked samples. As observed, the absorption of Ag0-PUL-Ag+ (Fig. 8(a) line a) increased progressively after the addition of ferrous fumarate tablet to the processed sample (Fig. 8(a) line b). With the addition of Fe2+-spiked samples at 20.0 and 80.0 μM, further absorbance increments could be clearly seen (Fig. 8(a) lines c and d). The photo images (Fig. 8(a) inset) showed recognizable color perturbations to the naked eye, which lends more credence to the observation from the UV–Vis results. The estimated concentration of Fe2+ in the ferrous fumarate tablet using Ag0-PUL-Ag+ and the associated recovery of spiked Fe2+ samples are shown in Fig. 8(b). The recovery of the spiked Fe2+ concentrations is acceptable according to the AOAC guidelines (AOAC 2016) [56]; hence, our method is suitable for Fe2+ monitoring in pharmaceutical tablets.

The estimated 18.46 μM of Fe2+ in ferrous fumarate tablets was converted to mass per tablet, taking into cognizance the dilution factors applied in the sample preparation stage in section 2.5. Accordingly, the concentration of Fe2+ in ferrous fumarate tablets was estimated at 15.68 mg per tablet. The calculation is shown in the supplementary materials. This was validated by analyzing the tablet using ferrous sulfate titration with potassium per manganate (VII) or KMnO4. This is a self-indicating redox titration, where the ferrous sulfate is acidified with dilute H2SO4 and then titrated against KMnO4 until the first faint pinkish color is observed as the end point. The calculated concentration of Fe2+ using the titration method yielded 16.37 mg per tablet. This value is very close to the 15.68 mg per tablet obtained using Ag0-PUL-Ag+. From the two results, using the titration method as the standard method, the relative error (RE) is estimated about 4.2%. This value is within the analytically acceptable RE range (≤10%) for method development [57,58]. This shows that Ag0-PUL-Ag+ can be applied for Fe2+ detection and quality control checks in drugs.

This work proposed a robust, sensitive, and selective colorimetric detection strategy for ferrous ion (Fe2+) detection in solution based on Ag0-PUL-Ag+, fabricated using an eco-friendly material called pullulan. The synthesis was carefully designed so that, at optimal synthesis conditions, the realized AgNPs contained silver in its two common oxidation states (Ag0 and Ag+). With the addition of the analyte (Fe2+), under support from buffer and pH choices, a redox reaction could be initiated between Ag+ and Fe2+. As a result, Fe2+ is oxidized to Fe3+, while Ag+ is concomitantly reduced to Ag0 or AgNPs, which are deposited on the initially present Ag0 in the PUL matrix. This engenders noticeable color perturbations and absorption spectra enhancement. The assay furnished a LOD of 0.15 μM with linearity within 1.0–20.0 and 20.0–125.0 μM. The Ag0-PUL-Ag+ probe was applied to Fe2+ detection in tap water and ferrous fumarate tablets as a quality control check. The method showed reliable accuracy and precision and was resistant against interference from a multitude of possible metal ions tested, including Fe3+. In comparison with other reported detection probes for Fe2+, this strategy offered simplicity and selectivity by virtue of the redox reaction-based detection strategy proposed. In all, an efficacious detection probe with far-reaching applications was demonstrated using the ordered polymeric architecture of pullulan biopolymer. To the best of our ability, this is one of the very few applications of pullulan biopolymer in MNP synthesis for analytical detection nanoprobes.
Titilope John Jayeoye: Conceptualization, Methodology, validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, review & editing, Writing final draft.
